picks.headline: Turnkey 750-acre ranch at lowest cost per acre
picks.reason: At $3,313/acre, this is the most land per dollar among fully developed properties — modern homes, utilities already in place, and proven year-round water. Class 6 soil means no row crops, but the property ran 2,500 head of cattle, so grazing-based self-sufficiency is well-documented. $2.485M is a capital ask, but infrastructure replacement cost alone would exceed that.

picks.headline: Live creek, lodge, and 313 acres of Ozark privacy
picks.reason: Year-round creek plus existing utilities and a lodge means no infrastructure build-out required — move in and start producing. Class 4 soil limits row cropping but pasture and timber are viable on 313 acres. At $5,910/acre the premium reflects built infrastructure; buyers get scale, seclusion, and genuine water security.

picks.headline: Ozark waterfall property with class 3 soil
picks.reason: Year-round creeks and a waterfall provide exceptional water security, and class 3 well-drained soil is the strongest agricultural rating in this candidate set for a sub-$400k property. Modern utilities and existing cabins eliminate first-year housing costs. The $9,912/acre is high for Arkansas but justified by dual water sources and immediate habitability.

picks.headline: Class 2 soil farm with lake and year-round ponds
picks.reason: USDA Class 2 soil is rare in this dataset — it supports genuine crop production alongside grazing and timber on 319 acres. Natural ponds and year-round water solve the most critical homestead dependency. At $8,516/acre the price is steep, and utilities and residential zoning should be confirmed, but the underlying land quality is the strongest in the set.

picks.headline: Year-round stream on statewide-importance farmland
picks.reason: Class 3 soil rated of statewide importance combined with a year-round stream is an unusually strong combination for a $45,900 parcel. Terrain is buildable at 6.2% slope with no flood risk. The main costs are septic and utility installation, which are predictable and manageable — not structural barriers.

picks.headline: Natural spring and class 3 farmland off-grid ready
picks.reason: A confirmed natural spring is one of the most valuable homestead assets in this entire list — it eliminates well-drilling risk and provides gravity-fed water potential. Class 3 agricultural soil supports small-scale farming, and 6.2% slopes are workable. Road access should be verified with seller, but at $78,900 for 8.78 acres with this water and soil profile, it competes with far pricier options.

picks.headline: 400-acre riverfront with springs and existing farm
picks.reason: Year-round springs, lakes, and river frontage give this property redundant water security across 400 acres. Existing improvements mean immediate occupancy. Class 4 soil is a real limitation for crop agriculture, so the $6,500/acre price is buying location, water, and infrastructure — not soil productivity. Best for buyers who can generate income beyond subsistence farming.

picks.headline: Paved access, year-round water, lowest Ozark price
picks.reason: Dogs Bluff South #7 offers the strongest combination in the budget parcel tier: paved access, explicit year-round water, electric on-site, no HOA, and 11.81 acres at $4,649/acre ($54,900 total). Class 4 soil rules out serious crop farming, but the infrastructure and access quality beat most other small parcels in this set. Sparse listing warrants an in-person visit to confirm utility specifics.

picks.headline: Paved road, 10 acres, lowest budget price per acre
picks.reason: At $4,431/acre with paved access, year-round water, and electric on-site, Shelton Road #4 is the best value among infrastructure-complete small parcels. Deep soil at 79 inches to bedrock means well drilling is viable. Class 4 soil limits farming to grazing and livestock; with 10.81 acres that's manageable for a small herd or chickens/pigs.

picks.headline: Nearly 10 acres, utilities in, build-ready today
picks.reason: Dogs Bluff South #17 is build-ready with electric and water available, no HOA, year-round water access, and grazing-suitable terrain on 9.83 acres at $47,900. Class 4 Bendavis-Poynor soil caps agricultural ambition, but the hunting viability and grazing suitability give practical self-sufficiency options. Solid entry-level homestead parcel.

picks.headline: 18 acres with water and electric at lowest per-acre
picks.reason: At $3,494/acre, Wildcat Hill SW offers the most land per dollar among parcels with confirmed utilities and year-round water — $62,900 total for 18 acres. The 12% slope and class 4 soil are real constraints: steep terrain limits where you can build and farm, but grazing is viable. Best for a buyer who can work with challenging terrain and wants maximum acreage on a tight budget.

picks.headline: 26 acres and cheapest price in the entire set
picks.reason: Ruby Ridge West #15 at $3,037/acre is the lowest per-acre price in the candidate pool, with 26.31 acres, electric access, stated year-round water, no HOA, and isolation for $79,900. Class 4 marginal soil and 12% slope limit farming and require thoughtful site selection for any build. Water source reliability is vague and should be the first thing verified — but for sheer acreage at this price, it has no competition.
